coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots from casein and their applications for sensitive detection of Hg2+ and biothiols and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shouming
Liu, Yang
Yang, Hong
Zhao, Kang
Li, Jianguo
Deng, Anping
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis strategy using casein as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources, and characterized by UV–vis spectrum, fluorescent spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and FT-IR, etc. The synthesized NSCDs displayed a blue emission under ultraviolet illumination with a quantum yield of 31.8%, and a good aqueous solubility, photostability and biocompatibility. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs could be selectively quenched by Hg2+, so NSCDs was used as an effective probe for the detection of Hg2+. The linear range and the limit of detection (LOD) of the fluorescent sensor based on NSCDs for the detection of Hg2+ were 0.01–0.25 μM and 6.5 nM, respectively. Spiked water samples were detected by the sensor with the recovery of 95.4–106.3% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.6–8.6%. It was also observed that the quenched NSCDs-Hg2+ system could be restored by the addition of biothiols such as l-cysteine (Lcy), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH), thus NSCDs-Hg2+ system was employed as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of biothiols. The linear range and LOD of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system were 1–10 μM and 23.6 nM for Lcy, 0.2–2.5 μM and 12.3 nM for Hcy, and 0.1–2.0 μM and 16.8 nM for GSH, respectively. The NSCDs-Hg2+ system was applied for the detection of biothiols in serum samples with satisfied results. In addition, the study in vitro imaging HeLa cells revealed that the synthesized NSCDs could be used as effective fluorescent probes in cellular imaging without noticeable cytotoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs)
Hg2+
Biothiols
Detection
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The detection of Hg2+and biothiols in real samples
The application of NSCDs in sensing Hg2+
The application of NSCDs in sensing biothiols
Application of NSCDs in cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of NSCDs in aqueous solution (inset: photographs of NSCDs solution under visible light and under UV light); (B) Emission spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths; (C) Upconversion fluorescence spectra of NSCDs at different excitation wavelength. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) XPS full scan spectrum of the NSCDs (inset: S2p spectrum); (B) FT-IR spectrum of the NSCDs; (C) XRD spectra of NSCDs (D) Typical TEM image of as-prepared NSCDs (inset: the particle size distribution histogram of the prepared NSCDs).

(A) Effects of pH of buffer solution on the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs, (B) Effects of KCl solutions with different ionic strengths on the stability of NSCDs; (C) Effects of the irradiation time with a UV lamp on the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs; (D) Viability of HeLa cells after incubation of 24 h with different concentrations of the NSCDs, as determined by the MTT assay. The error bars represent variations among six independent measurements.

(A) Relative fluorescence (F/F0) of aqueous NSCDs solutions in the presence of Hg2+ and other 15 metal ions at concentrations of 100 μM; (B) Relative fluorescence (F/F0) of aqueous NSCDs solutions before and after addition of various anion ions at concentrations of 100 μM; (C) The corresponding fluorescence intensities before (black) and after (red) the treatment with Hg2+ (100 μM); (D) Evolution of the fluorescence intensity over pH, recorded after the addition of 100 μM Hg2+ to NSCDs solution with different pH values. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Relative fluorescence (1-F/F0) of NSCDs as a function of Hg2+ concentration (0–100 μM) (inset: the linear detection range for Hg2+ was 0.01–0.25 μM).

The plots of F/F0 versus the concentration of biothiols (F0: the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs-Hg2+ system; F: the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs-Hg2+ system after adding biothiol). Inserted was the linear range of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system for the detection of biothiols. (A) The plot for Lcy. Linear detection range was 1–10 μM; (B) The plot for Hcy. Linear detection range was 0.2–2.5 μM; (C) The plot for GSH. Linear detection range was 0.1–2.0 μM.

Bright field images (A) and confocal fluorescence images (B) of HeLa cells upon 4 h incubation with NSCDs. Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells treated with10 μM mercury ions for 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 and 30 min, respectively (B, C, D, E, F, G). (H) Confocal fluorescence image after further incubation with 50 μM Lcy (Hcy or GSH) for 1 h. Fluorescence images were taken with 405 nm excitation. Scale bar: 250 μm.
The detection of Hg2+ in spiked water samples by NSCDs-based fluorescence sensor.
Comparison of analytical methods using CDs including heteroatom-co-doped CDs as fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots from casein and their applications for sensitive detection of Hg2+ and biothiols and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry & Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry & Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Nantong University, 9 Seyuan Rd., Nantong 226019, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy Sciences, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis strategy using casein as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources, and characterized by UV–vis spectrum, fluorescent spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and FT-IR, etc. The synthesized NSCDs displayed a blue emission under ultraviolet illumination with a quantum yield of 31.8%, and a good aqueous solubility, photostability and biocompatibility. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs could be selectively quenched by Hg2+, so NSCDs was used as an effective probe for the detection of Hg2+. The linear range and the limit of detection (LOD) of the fluorescent sensor based on NSCDs for the detection of Hg2+ were 0.01–0.25 μM and 6.5 nM, respectively. Spiked water samples were detected by the sensor with the recovery of 95.4–106.3% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.6–8.6%. It was also observed that the quenched NSCDs-Hg2+ system could be restored by the addition of biothiols such as l-cysteine (Lcy), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH), thus NSCDs-Hg2+ system was employed as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of biothiols. The linear range and LOD of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system were 1–10 μM and 23.6 nM for Lcy, 0.2–2.5 μM and 12.3 nM for Hcy, and 0.1–2.0 μM and 16.8 nM for GSH, respectively. The NSCDs-Hg2+ system was applied for the detection of biothiols in serum samples with satisfied results. In addition, the study in vitro imaging HeLa cells revealed that the synthesized NSCDs could be used as effective fluorescent probes in cellular imaging without noticeable cytotoxicity.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs)

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past two decades, quantum dots (QDs) become one of the most extensively optical sensing nanomaterials in bioimaging [1] and the detection of nucleic acids [2], enzymes [3], proteins [4], metal ions [5] and other small molecules [6] due to their high emission quantum yields and size tunable emission profiles. However, serious toxicity of QDs even at relatively low concentrations [7] and their superior photophysical features usually observed in organic solvent restrict tremendously their analytical potential [8], so researchers pay their attentions on methods to make these luminescent QDs water-soluble and biocompatible, such as surface passivation with protective layers [9,10]. But these protocols are compromised by reducing their photoluminescence efficiency and the time-consuming, complicated, expensive processes.

Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs), owing to their outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and robust chemical inertness [11,12], make them better than traditional quantum dots for many applications. Various methods have been demonstrated in preparation of FCDs, such as combustion [13], electrochemical oxidation [14], chemical oxidation [15], microwave heating [16] and so on. Among all of those preparation methods, thermal route is a promising and quite attractive one because it is particularly simple and efficient. In particular, to tune their electronic properties, surface and local chemistry, and to extend their applications, heteroatom (e.g., N, B, S, and Si) doping with or without surface modifications has also been actively investigated [17–20]. By using the precursors of l-cysteine and citric acid, N and S co-doped FCDs were prepared without using passivation reagents [21], or prepared from hair fiber [19], gentamycin sulfate [22] and rice [23]. Moreover, too much attention has been paid to their applications in bioimaging and sensing. Li et al. successfully prepared a novel high fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots by one-step microwave-assisted method and were successfully applied to probe Hg2+ in living cells [24]. Despite these good examples, a better strategy to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped FCDs with high quantum yield and further developing their potential applications is still needed.

Hg2+ is one of the most dangerous and ubiquitous pollutants, and represents a serious threat to the environment and human health [25]. Therefore, there has been an increasing interest to develop highly efficient methods for the sensitive and selective detection of trace amounts of Hg2+. Currently Hg2+ detection methods include atomic absorption/emission, spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, polarography and fluorescence assays [24,26–30]. Among them, fluorescence assays have been proven to be an alternative method for Hg2+ detection owing to their high sensitivity, good selectivity and fast analysis [31]. As one of fluorescence assays, CDs-based fluorescent nanoprobes with good water solubility has recently been actively explored for Hg2+ detection [24,32,33]. Although these CDs-based materials hold great promise in the detection of Hg2+, much work is still needed to explore this system and extend its applications.

l-cysteine (Lcy), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) are very common low-molecular-weight biothiols, playing crucial roles in biological processes such as reversible redox reactions and cellular functions [34]. Abnormal levels of them are linked with many ailments. Therefore, it is important to develop a sensitive and fast method for the determination of them. Recently, due to the features of high sensitivity, facile operation, real time and online detection, fluorescence detection has become more and more popular and much effort has been made to develop fluorescence sensors. The fluorescent sensors for the determination of biothiols based on CDs have also been developed in recent years [35–38].

Herein, we present a facile and general strategy to prepare nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (NSCDs) by thermal treatment of casein at a relatively low temperature. It should be pointed out that the starting materials of NSCDs synthesis are non-toxic, environmentally friendly and biocompatibility. The as-prepared NSCDs exhibit blue emission under ultraviolet illumination with a high quantum yield, good aqueous solubility, photostability, biocompatibility and cellular labeling capability. Fluorescent intensity of the NSCDs is quenched by Hg2+ and the quenched NSCDs-Hg2+ system can be restored by the addition of biothiols, thus the fluorescent sensors using NSCDs and NSCDs-Hg2+ as the probes are used for the detection of Hg2+ and biothiols. Also, the prepared NSCDs are applied in bioimaging.

Casein, mercuric chloride (HgCl2) and homocysteine (Hcy) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (Milwaukee, USA). Quinine sulfate, l-cysteine (Lcy), glutathione (GSH) and the other metal salts used here were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used as obtained without further purification until and unless stated.

In all preparations, high-purity deionized water (18.2 MΩ) from a Dura series (The Lab Corporation, USA) was used. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a spectrophotometer UV-2300 (Techcomp., Shanghai, China), and fluorescence spectra were taken on a spectrofluorometer F-2500 (Hitachi, Japan). The FTIR spectra were performed on a PerkinElmer Paragon 1000 FTIR spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a FEI Tecnai G-20 (FEI, Eindhoven, Netherlands), which was operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared by spraying a dispersion of NSCDs onto a Cu grid covered by a holey carbon film. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta-potential experiments were performed using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a K-Alpha XPS spectrometer (ThermoFisher, E. Grinstead, UK), using Al Ka X-ray radiation (1486.6 eV) for excitation. For XRD analysis, the diffraction pattern was obtained using a PANalytical X'Pert3 MRD diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA). The cellular fluorescence images were recorded by TCS SP5 II Confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica, Germany).

The luminescent NSCDs were synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis strategy. For a typical synthesis, 0.5 g of casein was heated in a stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the obtained brownish-black solid powder was dissolved with 5 mL water. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and then dialyzed against ultra-pure water through a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 48 h before use.

The quantum yield (QY) of NSCDs (35 μg mL−1) was measured according to the established procedure. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (literature quantum yield is 0.54 at 360 nm) was chosen as a standard. Absolute values were calculated using the standard reference sample that had a fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield value.

The detection of Hg2+ was performed in PB buffer (pH 7.4) at room temperature. 500 μL of NSCDs solution was added into a 1.5-mL centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 5 μL different concentrations of Hg2+. The FL emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1 min at room temperature. The selectivity for Hg2+ ion of NSCDs was confirmed by adding 5 μL of sixteen kinds of metal ions stock solutions (Cd2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, K+, Na+, Li+, Ag+, Hg2+ and Ni2+) 9 anionic ions (Cl−, SO4 2−, Ac2–-, NO3 −, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, CO3 2− and HCO3 −) with a concentration of 100 μM–500 μL of the NSCDs solution, respectively. Then the FL spectra were measured by the spectrofluorometer F-2500 for recording the fluorescence intensity.

Then, we investigated the effect of Lcy, Hcy or GSH on the FL recovery of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system. Herein, different amounts of analytes were added to the NSCDs-Hg2+ solution, shaken thoroughly and set for 1 min. Then the fluorescent spectra were measured by a fluorometer F-2500 and the maximum emission peak was used for quantitative analysis. All the samples were excited (λex) at 383 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of Hg2+and biothiols in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to strong fluorescence quenching of the NSCDs by the addition of Hg2+, based on this fluorescence sensor, we performed the experiment for the detection of Hg2+ in spiked tap, river and lake water samples. These samples were collected from our lab, a river near our campus and Jinji lake (Suzhou), respectively. After filtered through a 0.45 μm filter trap to discard insoluble, the water samples were spiked with Hg2+ at the concentrations of 50–200 nM and measured by NSCDs-based fluorescence sensor.

Based on the fluorescence restoration of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system by addition of biothiols (Lcy, Hcy, GSH) because of stronger binding force between Hg2+ and thiol group, we did experiment for the detection of biothiols in human serum samples. Two human sera were collected from healthy young volunteers and stored frozen until assay. The two serum samples were respectively spiked with biothiols at the concentrations of 0.5 and 1.0 μM, and then incubated for 1 h at room temperature. In order to eliminate the interferences of high proteins, the serum sample was mixed with acetonitrile at the radio of 1:1 to precipitate protein. After vortexing for 2 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm at 4 °C for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size filter membrane. The filtrate was diluted with PBS (pH 7.4) at appropriate times and detected by NSCDs-Hg2+ system.

The human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Gibco) in a 96-well culture plate at 37 °C incubator with 5% CO2. Prior to being tested, cells were seeded in 96 well plates at an initial density of 1 × 105 cells per mL for 24 h. Dilution of NSCDs was then added to DMEM medium and incubated for another 24 h. Afterwards, cells were washed twice with PBS followed by addition of 100 μL fresh medium and 20 μL 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diph-eny-2h-tetrazoliumbromide (MTT) stock suspension (5 mg mL−1) to each well. They were incubated for 4 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After removing all from the wells, we added 150 μL dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) to each well, mixed the solution thoroughly and incubated the plates at 37 °C for 10 min. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 490 nm with an enzyme mark instrument. The cell viability was estimated as: cell viability (%) = (OD treated/OD control) × 100%, where OD control and OD treated were obtained in the absence and presence of NSCDs, respectively. All the records were explained as average data with error bars.

The human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and the cells were seeded in the culture plate and cultured with 50 μM NSCDs. After incubation at 37 °C for 3 h, cells were washed three times with PBS, fixed with 4% p-formaldehyde for 30 min, and the fluorescence images were captured using TCS SP5 II Confocal laser scanning microscope.

Water-soluble fluorescent NSCDs were simply prepared by a one-step pyrolysis strategy using casein as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources. In order to get NSCDs with the highest quantum yields, the experiment conditions such as the reaction time and temperature were carefully optimized. The detailed optimization was presented in Electronic Supplemental Material (Fig. S1). At the optimal time (4 h) and temperature (180 °C), the as-prepared NSCDs solution was slightly yellow transparent liquid in visible light and a bright blue fluorescence was observed via irradiation with a UV lamp (inset in Fig. 1 A).

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of NSCDs were studied at room temperature. In Fig. 1A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NSCDs (black line) showed two characteristic absorption peaks at 270 nm and 375 nm, the former peak might be attributed to the n−π* transition of CO [39] and the latter was likely originated from the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states results in strong emission [21,40]. Meanwhile, the maximum excitation wavelength (blue line) and emission wavelength (red line) of the NSCD aqueous solution were 383 nm and 446 nm, respectively. When exposing NSCDs to a series of excitation wavelength ranging from 340 nm to 420 nm, the fluorescent intensity maximum firstly increased and then decreased, and the highest emission peak was obtained under the excitation at 380 nm (Fig. 1B). It exhibited the typical λex-dependent photoluminescence behavior of NSCDs, which was similar to other carbon dots reported previously [41–43]. Such properties were probably attributed to different sizes and a distribution of different surface states of NSCDs [24,43]. Besides exhibiting strong luminescence, NSCDs were shown to possess clear upconversion photoluminescence properties (Fig. 1C). When excited by long-wavelength light (from 700 nm to 800 nm), NSCDs exhibited the upconverted emissions located in the range from 440 nm to 450 nm. This property of NSCDs should be attributed to the multiphoton active process similar to previous reported carbon dots [44,45].

To examine the surface states and functional groups of the NSCDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), FT-IR spectra and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were employed. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the XPS full scan spectrum of NSCDs presented three predominant peaks at 531.08 eV, 400.08 eV, 285.08 eV and a weak peak at 167.58 eV, which corresponded to the binding energies of O1s, N1s, C1s and S2p, respectively [19]. These results indicated that as-prepared NSCDs were mainly composed of four elements of C, O, N and S. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s were deconvoluted into five peaks at 284.6, 285.7, 286.1, 287.4 and 288.6 eV (Fig. S2 A), which were attributed to the CC, CN, CO, CO and OCO groups, respectively. The high resolution N1s spectrum of the NSCDs (Fig. S2 B) exhibited two peaks at 399.6 and 400.5 eV, which were attributed to NC and NH, respectively. O1s spectrum (Fig. S2 C) presented OC (531.2 eV), OH (531.7 eV), OC (532.5 eV) and OCO (533.5 eV) peaks. S2p spectrum (Fig. S2 D) presented –C–SOx– (x = 2, 3, 4) peaks which were in accordance with previous reports [19,46]. In the FT-IR analysis of NSCDs (Fig. 2B), the follows were observed: the broad band at 3435–3127 cm−1 was attributed to OH and NH bonds [46,47], peak at 2972 cm−1 showed the CH stretching vibrations, and peak at 1665 cm−1 was attributed to the vibrational absorption band of CO [19]. In addition, the peak at 1403 cm−1 could be identified as the CNHC bonds and the peak at 1048 cm−1 was ascribed to –SO3 −, COC, and CO bonds [19,46]. All these results provided the evidence that oxygen containing functional groups, such as OH and COOH, present in the NSCDs, which not only improved the water solubility and facilitated the surface modification of NSCDs, but also could interact with metal ions [48]. To explore the formation of NSCDs composite, XRD patterns were also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, NSCDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak centered around 19° which might due to the little amorphous nature. This result is similar to those of reported carbon dots [49].

Further characterization studies such as TEM image showed the NSCDs had roughly spherical shapes and were well dispersed (Fig. 2D). The size distribution histogram obtained by counting about 100 particles indicated the size of NSCDs was between 2 and 3.2 nm with the average particle size of 2.7 nm (inset in Fig. 2D), which shed light on the explanation of the λex-dependent photoluminescence behavior of NSCDs. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis shows a single narrow peak at ∼ 3.75 nm (Fig. S3). Obviously, hydrodynamic diameter is larger than that from the TEM, which also suggests that the as-synthesized NSCDs are highly dispersible in aqueous solution. Using quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a standard, the quantum yield of the NSCDs was about 31.8%.

As the optical properties of CDs arise from particle surface states, thus different pH values may have distinct effects for the fluorescence intensity of CDs. To find an optimal pH value for the following experiment, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs was researched. The NSCDs exhibited remarkable fluorescence stability. The fluorescence intensities of NSCDs had less changeable between pH 4–10 (Fig. 3 A), especially in the physiological pH range. For a decrease in solution pH from 4 to 2, the protonation degree of the NSCDs gradually increases, producing higher concentrations of protonated nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the surface of the NSCDs and an increase in solution pH from 9 to 11, the deprotonation degree of the NSCDs gradually increases, which change the electron distributions. So the pH value of the solutions plays an important role in the particle surface states of NSCDs, which could affect the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs [50].

The NSCDs were also very stable in high-ionic strength solutions (Fig. 3B), which was of benefit for the use of NSCDs in salt solutions. Additionally, the NSCDs showed excellent photostability, as the photoluminescence intensity did not change even after continuous excitation with a UV lamp (Fig. 3C). All these features made NSCDs good candidates for bioimaging or other biomedical applications.

For bioimaging, it is required that the selected fluorescent marker has not only optical merits but also low cytotoxicity [51]. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of NSCDs, MTT assays were carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, the cell viability was defined as 100% in absence of NSCDs. When 10–200 μM of NSCDs was added and incubated with the HeLa cells for 24 h, it was observed that decline of the cell viability was less than 5%, which clearly demonstrated the intrinsically low toxicity of the NSCDs to the cells (without any further functionalization) [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of NSCDs in sensing Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensing ability of NSCDs toward cationic and anionic ions, changes in the fluorescence properties of NSCDs upon the addition of various cationic and anionic ions (100 μM) were measured. In this study, we found that the fluorescence of the NSCDs could be effectively quenched when 100 μM Hg2+ was added into the solution (Fig. 4 A) and the quenching process is completed within 1 min (Fig. S4); while under the same conditions, the addition of other 15 metal ions including Cd2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, K+, Na+, Li+, Ag+, Ni2+ and 9 anionic ions including Cl−, SO4 2−, Ac2–, NO3 −, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, CO3 2− and HCO3 − at concentration of 100 μM, resulted in small or no obvious fluorescence changes (Fig. 4A and B). For NSCDs solutions containing different ions at the concentration 100 μM, when Hg2+ (100 μM) was added, the fluorescence intensity was sharply decreased (Fig. 4C). These results revealed that the NSCDs probe presented an excellent selectivity for Hg2+ sensing over the other metal ions.

For Hg2+ sensing, the effects of pH on the fluorescence response to Hg2+ of NSCDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4D, NSCDs showed the highest fluorescence response toward Hg2+ ion under the pH 7.0, and further increasing the pH value caused a low quenching efficiency because in the solution with high pH value, Hg2+ was easily hydrolyzed [52] and might be complexed by OH− instead of the NSCDs under typical conditions [53]. For the potential application in biological systems, the pH 7.4 was chosen as an optimum experimental condition.

Under the optimized conditions, different concentrations of Hg2+ solution were added to the aqueous solution of NSCDs and the fluorescence emission intensity was measured to study the sensitivity of the NSCDs sensor. With the increase of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs at around 446 nm decreased gradually (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 5 , the value of (F0−F)/F0 increased gradually with increasing Hg2+ concentration and a linear calibration in the range from 0.01 to 0.25 μM was obtained (Y = 0.00124 + 0.51X, R = 0.996), where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of NSCDs at 446 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. And the limit of detection was estimated to be 6.5 nM based on three times signal-to-noise ratio, which was lower than the maximum allowed concentration of Hg2+ in drinking water according to United States Environment Protection Agency [54].

Three water samples spiked with Hg2+ at the concentrations of 50–200 nM were detected by NSCDs-based fluorescence sensor and the results were presented in Table 1 . It was found that there was no detectable Hg2+ in all three real samples, and the recoveries of the Hg2+ from spiked samples were in range of 95.4–106.3% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.6–8.6%. These results demonstrated that the NSCDs-based fluorescence sensor was applicable for the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples.

In the last years, many analytical methods using CDs including heteroatom-co-doped CDs as fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+ have been developed [41,52,55–59]. The fluorescence quenching of NSCDs may result from the chelating reaction of Hg2+ with NSCDs through sulfur and the functional groups on the NSCDs surface such as NH2 and COOH. The comparison of these methods including our NSCDs-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ was presented in Table 2 . It was seen that the LOD value achieved in our method was lower than that in most reports, indicating high sensitivity of our method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of NSCDs in sensing biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: When biothiols including Lcy, Hcy and GSH were added to NSCDs-Hg2+ system, the fluorescence intensity was dramatically increased (Fig. S6) due to the thiol groups as strong Hg2+ chelators were able to remove Hg2+ from NSCDs surfaces through the formation of HgS bonds [36,37]. To evaluate the sensing system's specificity for biothiols detection, the fluorescence analyses of the NSCDs-Hg2+ were carried out in the present of other amino acids including arginine (Arg), tyrosine (Tyr), proline (Pro), leucine (Leu), phenylalanine (Phen), glycine (Gly), tryptophane (Trp), threonine (Thr) and valine (Val). As shown in Fig. S7, the quenched fluorescence intensity was restored immediately (about 1 min) upon the addition of Lcy, Hcy, and GSH into the system of NSCDs-Hg2+, whereas no obvious signal change was observed for other substances, so the high selectivity of this probe provided a potential for the efficient detection of biothiols.

In this work, Lcy, Hcy and GSH were chosen as the examples to demonstrate the restoration ability of biothiols to the NSCDs-Hg2+ system. For the potential application in biological systems, the following detection was conducted when the pH of reaction system was 7.4. As shown in Fig. 6 (A,B,C), with the concentration of Lcy, Hcy and GSH increased, the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system enhanced gradually, and even restored to its original intensity with the increasing concentration of Lcy, Hcy and GSH (Fig. S6). The ratio of fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of biothiols exhibited a linear response to biothiols concentration (Fig. 6, inserted). The linear range of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system for Lcy, Hcy and GSH were found to be 1–10 μM (R = 0.992, Fig. 6A), 0.2–2.5 μM (R = 0.989, Fig. 6B) and 0.1–2.0 μM (R = 0.997, Fig. 6C) with the LOD values (S/N = 3) of 23.6 nM, 12.3 nM and 16.8 nM, respectively.

Two human sera respectively spiked with Lcy, Hcy and GSH at the concentration of 0.5 μM and 1.0 μM were detected NSCDs-Hg2+ system. The resulted were presented in Table S1. It was seen that there was some content of biothiols existed in the blank sample without spiking. The recoveries of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system for the detection of Lcy, Hcy and GSH in spiked serum samples were 94.3–115.2% with the RSD in range of 4.92–8.86%. These results demonstrated the applicability of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system for the detection of Lcy, Hcy and GSH in serum samples.

Recently analytical methods based on the probes of the fluorescent restoration system for the detection of biothiols have been reported [36,60–63]. In these methods, different fluorescent materials such as gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs), etc. have been utilized. The comparison of these methods including ours for the detection of biothiols was presented in Table S2. It was seen from Table S2 that our method not only detected all three biothiols in the serum sample, but also displayed the lower LOD values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NSCDs in cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa cells were seeded in the culture plate and cultured with 50 μM NSCDs in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS. After 4 h of incubation at 37 °C with 5% CO2, the photographs of the HeLa cells were captured by a laser scanning confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. 7 B, obviously the HeLa cells became quite bright owing to the strong fluorescence from the NSCDs, which confirmed the NSCDs could penetrate the cells membrane and translocate into cells by endocytosis.

In this work, the quenching of the Hg2+ on NSCDs and the fluorescence restoration of the NSCDs-Hg2+ system by the addition of biothiols were also demonstrated by imaging performance. It is known that not all concentrations of Hg2+ significantly accelerate apoptosis of cells [64]. It was found that the HeLa cells incubated with NSCDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 7B). After being treated by adding 10 μM Hg2+, with the time increase from 5 min to 30 min, the intracellular fluorescence intensity in these cells was getting weaker (Fig. 7B–G), demonstrating that Hg2+ diffused into the cell and bound with NSCDs. We also treated the cells with different concentrations of Hg2+ (2, 5 and 10 μM) to examine their apoptosis in 30 min. It was observed that with the concentration of Hg2+ increase, the intracellular fluorescence intensity was decreased (Fig. S8). When the cells loaded with NSCDs-Hg2+ were further treated with Lcy, GSH or Hcy, strong blue fluorescence was restored (Fig. 7H). These findings revealed that the NSCDs could be potential employed for high selective visual detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in living cells.

We have developed a simple, fast, one-step method to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) by a one-step pyrolysis strategy using casein as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources. The as-prepared NSCDs not only exhibited good dispersibility, strong photoluminescence and outstanding photostability but also displayed a remarkably rapid, sensitive and selective response toward Hg2+ over other metal ions. The linear range of the fluorescent Hg2+ sensor using NSCDs as probe was 0.01–0.25 μM with LOD of 6.5 nM. The recoveries of this sensor for Hg2+ in spiked water samples were 95.4–106.3% with RSD of 3.6–8.6%. Based on the fluorescence restoration of the NSCD-Hg2+ system by addition biothiols, another fluorescent sensor using NSCDs-Hg2+ system as probe for sensitive and selective detection of Lcy, Hcy and GSH in serum samples was also developed. In addition, the synthesized NSCDs were effectively applied in vitro imaging HeLa cells without noticeable cytotoxicity. We believe that the developed approach potentially opens up a simple and environment-friendly route to prepare heteroatom-co-doped CDs with broad application prospects.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, contact No. 21075087 and 21175097) and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions for financial support of this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online Data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.01.037.
